title: Mark 10
subtitle: On the Road to Jerusalem: Divorce, Children, Wealth, Service, Sight

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — Marriage and Divorce; Children Welcomed

heb.paragraph: At the beginning of creation — Jesus grounds the marriage ethic not in Mosaic law but in creation order. The appeal to Genesis 1 goes behind the Mosaic concession to the original divine design. The creation ethic is prior to and normative over all subsequent law.

places.name: Region of Judea and across the Jordan (Perea)
places.coords: East of the Jordan, common route from Galilee to Jerusalem
places.text: The journey through Perea marks the deliberate turn toward the Passion. Jesus’s movement into Judean territory begins the geographic arc that will end at Golgotha.

refs.note: God made them male and female — Jesus grounds the marriage ethic in creation order, not Mosaic concession.
A man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife — the one-flesh permanence Jesus quotes.
The Mosaic divorce certificate — Jesus reinterprets it as a concession to hardness of heart, not divine ideal.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "Because of your hardness of heart Moses wrote you this law" — Jesus appeals to the creation order as theologically prior to Mosaic concession. Marcus notes this argument-form (from creation prior to Torah) has parallels in the Qumran Damascus Document (CD 4:19-5:6), which similarly appeals to creation-order marriage norms against what it regards as corruptions in contemporary practice. The technique — going behind a problematic law to its pre-law foundation — was a live exegetical strategy.

Marcus: "He was indignant" (ēganaktēsen) — the same word appears in 10:41 when the ten are indignant at James and John. The disciples gatekeep children from Jesus; the disciples gatekeep positions of glory from each other. Both forms of gatekeeping are rebuked. Marcus argues that the child-blessing episode is not merely about affection for children but is a statement about kingdom-entry conditions: the child, having no claims, no status, and no leverage, exemplifies the posture of one who receives the kingdom as a gift.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The Judean setting (10:1) marks the final geographic transition: Jesus is now in the territory that will lead to Jerusalem. The Pharisees' divorce question is presented as a "test" (peirazō) — the same word used for the wilderness temptation (1:13 is implied) and the demand for a sign (8:11). The narrator consistently uses this word to signal that questioners are not seeking truth but trying to entrap. Jesus's answer through the creation-order argument places him above the test — he refuses the premise.

Rhoads: The children episode is placed between two texts about the kingdom's demands (marriage, wealth). Its function in the sequence is to demonstrate the kingdom's entry condition by contrast: wealth and social standing are obstacles (10:17-22); child-like reception is the model. Rhoads argues the sequence is not accidental — the juxtaposition of the impossible camel (10:25) with the blessed child (10:15) encodes the Gospel's core paradox: the way in is for those who have nothing to offer.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The teaching on divorce is, for Chrysostom, a return to origins — Jesus appeals to Genesis 1-2 over the Mosaic concession of Deuteronomy 24. The Pharisees cite the law; Jesus cites the creation order. Bede observes that "hardness of heart" (v5) is not a neutral anthropological observation but a theological diagnosis: the concession was given because of a deficiency in the people, not a permission built into the created order. The private explanation to the disciples (vv.10-12) is, for Origen, the interior teaching that completes the public statement — the standard applies symmetrically to both parties.

The blessing of the children is, for Chrysostom, the immediate contextual answer to the divorce dispute: children are the living argument against the disposability of covenant relationship. Jesus's rebuke of the disciples is notable in Mark for its emotional force — "he was indignant." The saying "the kingdom of God belongs to such as these" is, for Origen, not a romanticization of childhood but a theological point about receptivity: children receive gifts they cannot earn, which is the structure of all grace. The command to "receive the kingdom of God like a little child" is, for Ambrose, the condition of entry.

Moses permitted divorce because of your hardness of heart — the concession principle. Mosaic law accommodates human sin without endorsing it. The appeal to creation exposes the difference between divine design and pastoral concession.
He was indignant — the same righteous anger (ēganaktēsen) as 10:41. The disciples’ gatekeeping of children provokes it. The kingdom’s entry condition is child-like reception, not adult qualification.

Let no one separate — the prohibition is addressed to human will, not to God’s providence. Calvin acknowledges the church must have processes for genuine dissolution while maintaining the creational ideal.
The children episode rebukes those who think the kingdom is for the capable and qualified. The entry requirement is precisely the absence of qualification — willingness to receive without earning.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Tested him (peirazō) — the same word as the wilderness temptation. Any answer will alienate either the conservative school of Shammai or the liberal school of Hillel. Jesus refuses the premise and appeals to creation.
If she divorces her husband — Jewish law did not grant women the right to initiate divorce. Jesus’s extension reflects either a Roman legal context or a principle of equal moral accountability.

hist.context: The movement from Galilee into Judea (10:1) is the geographic turning point: the journey to Jerusalem has begun. The Pharisees’ divorce test receives a creation-order answer that goes behind Moses. The children episode immediately follows — kingdom entry requires becoming like a child: the opposite of the disciples’ gatekeeping.

sections.header: Verses 17–45 — The Rich Young Man; Third Passion Prediction; The Ransom

heb.paragraph: The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many (v.45) — lytron anti pollōn. Lytron is the price paid to free a slave or captive. The anti indicates substitution: his life in the place of many. This is the clearest substitutionary atonement statement in Mark.

refs.note: He will see the result of his suffering… he bore the sin of many — the servant song that 10:45 directly echoes.
The Son of Man receiving authority — here re-described in servant terms: the Son of Man comes not to be served but to give his life.
A ransom for his life — the legal background for lytron: the payment that redeems a life otherwise forfeit.

echoes.source_context: The Servant makes himself a guilt offering, bears the sin of many, and makes intercession for transgressors. His death accomplishes what no animal sacrifice could.
echoes.connection: Jesus declares that the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many. The 'many' directly echoes Isaiah 53's repeated emphasis on the Servant's work for 'the many.'
echoes.significance: This is Jesus' own interpretation of his death in Servant Song categories. The ransom language connects substitution (dying in place of) with liberation (paying the price to free).

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "A ransom for many" (lytron anti pollōn) — Marcus's most sustained exegetical argument is that this phrase is the culmination of Mark's use of Isa 53 throughout the Gospel. The "many" (pollōn) echoes Isa 53:11-12 ("he will justify many"; "he bore the sin of many"). The lytron (ransom) draws on Exod 21:30 and the broader Levitical system of substitutionary payment. Marcus argues that the ransom saying is not a Pauline interpolation but the theological fulcrum of Mark's own narrative: it defines why the Son of Man goes to Jerusalem.

Marcus: "The cup I drink" and "the baptism I am baptized with" — both metaphors draw on OT imagery. The cup of divine wrath (Isa 51:17-22; Jer 25:15-17; Ps 75:8) and the flood/baptism of overwhelming disaster (Ps 42:7; 69:1-2; Isa 43:2) are two ways of describing the coming passion. Marcus argues that the double metaphor prepares for the Gethsemane prayer (14:36: "take this cup from me") and the cry of dereliction (15:34) — both moments when the passion-language becomes literal.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The rich young man is the only person in Mark who approaches Jesus correctly (running, kneeling), asks the right question (eternal life), receives the right answer, and refuses it. His characterization is the most complex of any minor figure: virtuous, sincere, and ultimately deficient in the one thing that counts. Rhoads argues that the scene functions as a narrative mirror for the reader: the right approach + the right question + the right answer still leaves the person unchanged if the one demand is refused. Hearing is not enough.

Rhoads: "Jesus was leading the way, and the disciples were astonished, and those who followed were afraid." The visual portrait — Jesus striding ahead, disciples astonished, followers afraid — is one of the most evocative in the Gospel. The narrator gives us a spatial image: Jesus ahead, the gap between him and the disciples visible. Rhoads reads the spatial description as characterization: Jesus's deliberateness and the disciples' inability to keep up is the relationship in miniature. His pace is the pace of the narrative's inevitability.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The rich young man is, for Chrysostom, the clearest Gospel example of the collision between genuine religious aspiration and the final cost of discipleship. He has kept the commandments; he lacks only one thing — and that one thing is everything. Bede notes that Jesus "looked at him and loved him" before delivering the hard saying: the love precedes the demand, which is the pattern of all divine call. The disciples' astonishment ("Who then can be saved?") is answered with the saying about the camel and the eye of a needle — which Origen reads as a statement of absolute human impossibility met by absolute divine possibility.

The third passion prediction (vv.32-34) is immediately followed by the request of James and John for seats of glory — the starkest juxtaposition in Mark between the teacher's direction and the disciples' understanding. Chrysostom says that the cup and baptism Jesus refers to are his death and the consequent sharing in his passion — the disciples say "We can" without knowing what they are affirming. The teaching on servant-leadership (vv.41-45) culminates in the great ransom saying: "the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many." Ambrose says this is the doctrinal core of the entire Gospel.
Jesus looked at him and loved him — the only explicit statement in Mark that Jesus loved an individual who is not a disciple. The love and the demand are inseparable.

The ransom for many — the theological climax of Mark’s journey section. Every teaching on greatness and self-denial has been building to this: Jesus himself is the paradigm. The cross is not an accident; it is the ransom.
Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone — the question is not a denial of divinity but an invitation: if I am good, consider what that implies about who I am.
All things are possible with God — the salvation of the wealthy person requires divine miracle, not moral reformation. Entrance of the rich into the kingdom is a resurrection-level act of grace.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The most detailed passion prediction: seven elements — delivered to chief priests and scribes, condemned, handed to Gentiles, mocked, spit upon, flogged, killed, raised. Each is fulfilled in chs.14-16.
Anti pollōn — the preposition anti consistently means substitution in NT Greek. The death is a specific exchange: his life in the place of many, echoing Isa 53:11-12.

hist.context: The third and most detailed passion prediction (10:33-34) is bracketed by two failure stories: the rich young man who cannot surrender wealth (10:17-22) and James and John who request the best seats (10:35-40). Mark 10:45 is the theological climax of the entire pre-Passion narrative: the ransom saying defines why Jesus is going to Jerusalem.

heb.paragraph: Son of David, have mercy on me — the first use of this messianic title by a human being in Mark. The blind beggar uses it twice, insistently. Where the religious authorities demanded silence and the disciples argued about greatness, a blind beggar sees clearly who Jesus is.

places.name: Jericho
places.coords: Jordan Valley; 250m below sea level; last city before Jerusalem
places.text: Jericho is the gateway to Jerusalem on the eastern approach. After Jericho the road climbs steeply through the Judean wilderness to Jerusalem (from 250m below sea level to 750m above). Bartimaeus is healed at the foot of the final ascent.

refs.note: Then will the eyes of the blind be opened — the eschatological healing Bartimaeus receives.
The LORD gives sight to the blind — what YHWH does, Jesus does.
The eternal Davidic throne — the “Son of David” title rooted in the Davidic covenant.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "Son of David, have mercy on me" — this is the first human use of the Son of David title in Mark (the scribes discuss it in 12:35-37, but no one addresses Jesus by it until Bartimaeus). Marcus argues that the Davidic messianology implicit in the title is being deliberately invoked: Bartimaeus approaches Jesus as the heir of David's royal-healing tradition. The blindness recalls 2 Sam 5:8 and the legend of David's Jerusalem conquest, where the blind and lame were associated with the city's pre-Davidic state.

Marcus: "He followed Jesus along the road" (en tē hodō) — Marcus notes that hodos (road, way) is a structural keyword in Mark. The disciples were called on the road (1:16-20); the parables were explained on the road (8:27); the passion predictions were given on the road (8:27; 9:33-34; 10:32). In the Acts of the Apostles, "the Way" (hē hodos) becomes the earliest name for the Christian movement. Bartimaeus's following Jesus on the road at the end of ch.10 is, narratively, his entry into the story's ongoing movement toward Jerusalem and the cross.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: Bartimaeus closes a structural bracket opened by the blind man at Bethsaida (8:22-26). Both healings are done with unusual physical detail; both frame the central section of the Gospel (8:22-10:52) which contains all three passion predictions and all three failed-disciple responses. The bracket is interpretive: the Gospel's central section is flanked by two blind men receiving sight. The disciples who receive the light of three explicit passion predictions remain effectively blind. Bartimaeus sees; they do not.

Rhoads: "Throwing his cloak aside" — the single action is one of the most economical characterizations in the Gospel. The cloak is the begging equipment, the night covering, the one possession. To throw it aside before receiving the healing is an act of absolute trust: he has abandoned the old life before the new one has been granted. Rhoads argues that this detail does more characterization work than paragraphs of interior monologue would: we see who Bartimaeus is entirely through what he does.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Bartimaeus is, for Chrysostom, the model disciple at the end of the discipleship section (chs 8-10): blind, marginalized, silenced by the crowd — yet he cries out more loudly and receives sight. His cry "Son of David, have mercy on me" is, for Origen, the messianic acclamation the disciples have been slow to make openly. Bede notes that unlike the two-stage healing of the blind man at Bethsaida (8:22-26), Bartimaeus receives immediate, complete sight — the contrast is between those who need gradual revelation and those whose faith is already formed. His following of Jesus "along the road" is, for Ambrose, the literal enactment of the disciple's calling.

Jesus's command "Call him" and the crowd's sudden reversal — from silencing to encouraging — is, for Theophylact, a figure of the church's mediation: those who had been an obstacle become the means of the approach. The throwing off of the cloak before coming to Jesus is, for Bede, the casting aside of every impediment — possessions, reputation, security — in the urgency of coming to Christ. The final exchange — "What do you want me to do for you?" / "Rabbi, I want to see" — mirrors the Sons of Zebedee's request (10:36) but with a radically different content: one asks for glory, the other for sight.
Son of David, have mercy on me — the messianic title rejected by the crowd is exactly what Jesus responds to. Bartimaeus’s persistence is faith in action.
He followed Jesus along the road — the road to Jerusalem, where the Son of David will enter in triumph and die on a cross.

He shouted all the more — persistence in prayer in the face of human discouragement is one of the marks of genuine faith. Those whose faith is superficial are silenced; those whose need is genuine shout louder.
Your faith has healed you — the faith that healed Bartimaeus was not the content of his theological knowledge but the direction of his trust, entirely toward Jesus.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Bartimaeus, son of Timaeus — the named individual suggests a known figure in the early church. The Aramaic name is translated for Gentile readers.

Was following him in the Way (en tē hodō) — hodos (road/way). In Acts, “the Way” becomes the name for the Christian movement. Bartimaeus is the first person explicitly said to follow Jesus on the Way.

hist.context: Bartimaeus is Mark’s most individualized healing recipient — the only one given a name and its meaning. His persistence against the crowd’s silencing, his immediate response to the call (throwing off his cloak — his begging equipment), and his following Jesus on the road make him the model disciple of the entire journey section. His healing is the last act before the Triumphal Entry.

ppl.name: Bartimaeus
ppl.role: Blind beggar at Jericho; model disciple
ppl.text: The only healing recipient in Mark given a name. His persistence, throwing off the cloak, and following on the road make him the exemplar of the faith Mark 10 has been describing.

ppl.name: James and John
ppl.role: Sons of Zebedee; seeking seats of glory
ppl.text: Their request for the best seats (10:35-37) immediately after the third passion prediction illustrates the disciples’ persistent failure to understand the kingdom’s values.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.ESVFor even the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.KJVFor even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many.NASBFor even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many.NLTFor even the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve others and to give his life as a ransom for many.

src.title: Isaiah 53:11-12 (Servant Song)
src.quote: By his knowledge my righteous servant will justify many… he bore the sin of many — the servant song that Mark 10:45 directly echoes.
src.note: Confirms the Isaiah 53 background for the ransom saying and Jesus’s self-understanding as the Servant of YHWH.

rec.text: Anselm’s satisfaction theory of atonement is built substantially on Mark 10:45. The ransom for many is the satisfaction of divine justice that no human being could offer — requiring one both human and divine.

rec.who: N.T. Wright, The Day the Revolution Began (2016)
rec.text: Wright argued the ransom saying should be understood in Exodus-liberation terms: Jesus ransoms the people from captivity to sin as YHWH ransomed Israel from Egypt. The two readings are complementary.

rows.label: Three Tests of True Greatness on the Road
Bartimaeus as Mirror: Sight After the Disciples’ Blindness

hebtext.note: At the beginning of creation — Jesus grounds the marriage ethic not in Mosaic law but in creation order. The appeal to Genesis 1 goes behind the Mosaic concession to the original divine design. The creation ethic is prior to and normative over all subsequent law.

The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom for many (v.45) — lytron anti pollōn. Lytron is the price paid to free a slave or captive. The anti indicates substitution: his life in the place of many. This is the clearest substitutionary atonement statement in Mark.

Son of David, have mercy on me — the first use of this messianic title by a human being in Mark. The blind beggar uses it twice, insistently. Where the religious authorities demanded silence and the disciples argued about greatness, a blind beggar sees clearly who Jesus is.

tx.title: "The Pharisees came and tested him" — with or without "came to him"
tx.content: NA28reads Pharisaioi proseltho̱ntes (coming to him, the Pharisees); some MSS omit proseltho̱ntes as redundant.
tx.note: Minor variation with no impact on meaning. The fuller reading is better attested.

tx.title: "How hard it is to enter the kingdom of God" — with or without wealth clause
tx.content: NA28/UBS5reads the shorter text;later MSSadd "for those who trust in riches" (tois pepoitho̱tas epi chrēmasin), making the warning specifically about wealth rather than universally about kingdom entry.
tx.note: The shorter reading is original and theologically harder: it makes all kingdom entry difficult, not merely entry for the wealthy. The addition softens the statement by limiting it to those with attachment to riches.

positions.name: Substitutionary view
positions.proponents: Anti pollōn (for many) uses the preposition of substitution. The lytron pays what the many owe. Jesus takes the place of those who are ransomed.

positions.name: Liberation/Exodus view
positions.proponents: The ransom language draws on the Exodus pattern: YHWH ransomed Israel from Egypt without paying a price to Pharaoh. The liberation is real without requiring a payment to a captor.

themes.note: Mark 10 is the discipleship chapter: the cost of following Jesus (wealth, ambition, status) is defined against the ransom saying (10:45) as Jesus deliberately walks toward Jerusalem.

coaching.tip: 'Moses permitted you to divorce... because your hearts were hard' (v. 5). Jesus distinguishes permission from intention. Creation's design—'one flesh'—precedes Mosaic concession.
'The Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (v. 45). Mark's thesis statement: Jesus defines greatness by sacrifice, not status.

prayer_prompt: As Jesus says 'whoever wants to become great among you must be your servant,' consider asking God whether your ambition is driving you toward prominence or toward the people who need you most.
